name: Clytemnestra
description: Clytemnestra, daughter of King Tyndareus of Sparta and Leda, was the wife of Agamemnon, King of Mycenae, and sister to Helen of Troy. Her life was marked by tragedy and vengeance. Agamemnon sacrificed their daughter, Iphigenia, to appease Artemis and ensure favorable winds for the Trojan War. This act fueled Clytemnestra's burning hatred for her husband. While Agamemnon was away at war for ten years, she took Aegisthus, Agamemnon's cousin, as her lover. Together, they plotted Agamemnon's murder upon his return. Clytemnestra, a powerful and determined woman, carried out the assassination, killing both Agamemnon and Cassandra, the Trojan princess he brought home as a concubine. She ruled Mycenae with Aegisthus until their deaths at the hands of Orestes, her son, who avenged his father's murder, fulfilling Apollo's command. Clytemnestra represents a complex figure of betrayal, maternal grief, and righteous anger, challenging traditional notions of female passivity in Greek mythology.
subtitle: Queen of Mycenae and vengeful wife.
achievements: Maintained power during the decade of Agamemnon's absence

keyMyths.title: Sacrifice of Iphigenia
keyMyths.description: Agamemnon sacrificed his daughter Iphigenia to appease Artemis, enraging Clytemnestra and setting the stage for her revenge. This event became the catalyst for her hatred and eventual murder of Agamemnon.

keyMyths.title: Murder of Agamemnon
keyMyths.description: Upon Agamemnon's return from Troy, Clytemnestra, with the help of Aegisthus, murdered him in his bath, avenging Iphigenia's death and seizing control of Mycenae. This act solidified her reputation as a powerful and vengeful queen.

keyMyths.title: Orestes' Revenge
keyMyths.description: Years later, Orestes, Agamemnon's son, returned to Mycenae and, driven by Apollo's command, killed Clytemnestra and Aegisthus to avenge his father's murder. This act completed the cycle of violence within the House of Atreus.

sources.author: Aeschylus
sources.description: A tragedy detailing Agamemnon's return and murder.
sources.text: A tragedy detailing Agamemnon's return and murder.

sources.author: Homer
sources.description: Contains references to Clytemnestra's role in Agamemnon's death.
sources.text: Contains references to Clytemnestra's role in Agamemnon's death.

sources.author: Euripides
sources.description: A tragedy focusing on Electra's role in avenging her father.
sources.text: A tragedy focusing on Electra's role in avenging her father.

associations.name: Serpent
associations.significance: Represents treachery, cunning, and revenge, mirroring Clytemnestra's hidden plots and deadly nature.

associations.name: Axe
associations.significance: Symbolizes Clytemnestra's violent act of murder against Agamemnon, representing her power and rage.

associations.name: Vengeance
associations.significance: The driving force behind Clytemnestra's actions, representing her determination to avenge her daughter Iphigenia.

associations.name: Mycenae
associations.significance: The kingdom Clytemnestra rules in Agamemnon's absence, highlighting her ambition and political power.

associations.name: Betrayal
associations.significance: Represents the broken bond between Clytemnestra and Agamemnon, leading to irreversible consequences and tragedy.

associations.name: Crimson
associations.significance: Associated with blood and the violent acts Clytemnestra commits, symbolizing death and retribution.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Like Clytemnestra, Gudrun seeks vengeance for the death of her loved ones, showcasing a similar theme of retribution.
Both Clytemnestra and Medb are powerful queens who exert significant influence and demonstrate strong will.
Both Clytemnestra and Hatshepsut assumed power in the absence of a male ruler, demonstrating ambition and leadership.

worshipPractices.name: Dramatic Performances
worshipPractices.description: Clytemnestra's story is primarily explored through dramatic performances, particularly Greek tragedies, where her actions are debated and analyzed for their moral implications. These performances served as a form of cultural reflection on justice, revenge, and the roles of women in society.

cultural.modernLegacy: Clytemnestra remains a powerful figure in modern literature, film, and theater, often portrayed as a complex character driven by justifiable rage and a desire for justice. Her story continues to resonate with contemporary audiences, prompting discussions about gender roles, power dynamics, and the consequences of violence.

extendedContent.title: Early Life and Marriage
extendedContent.content: Clytemnestra's early life was marked by familial strife and divine intervention. As the daughter of King Tyndareus and Leda, her parentage is intertwined with the myth of Zeus and the swan, adding a layer of complexity to her identity. Her marriage to Agamemnon, King of Mycenae, was initially a political alliance but soon became a source of deep-seated resentment. The seeds of her later actions were sown early in the Trojan saga when Agamemnon, to appease the goddess Artemis and secure favorable winds for his fleet, sacrificed their daughter Iphigenia. This horrific act left an indelible scar on Clytemnestra, fueling her burning desire for revenge. The sacrifice of Iphigenia is a pivotal moment, transforming Clytemnestra from a queen into a figure consumed by grief and a thirst for retribution, setting the stage for the tragic events that would unfold upon Agamemnon's return from Troy.

extendedContent.title: Greatest Quests: Justice and Revenge
extendedContent.content: Clytemnestra's 'quest' is not one of physical journey but of emotional and political maneuvering. While Agamemnon was away at the Trojan War, she ruled Mycenae with a firm hand, consolidating her power and plotting her revenge. She took Aegisthus, Agamemnon's cousin, as her lover, further solidifying her position and gaining an ally in her schemes. Upon Agamemnon's triumphant return with Cassandra, Clytemnestra feigned joy, concealing her true intentions. In a calculated act of violence, she murdered both Agamemnon and Cassandra, an act driven by a complex mix of grief, rage, and a desire for justice for her daughter's death. Her actions challenge traditional notions of heroism, as she operates outside the bounds of conventional morality, becoming a symbol of female agency and resistance against patriarchal power structures. Her quest is one for personal justice, achieved through ruthless means, making her a morally ambiguous figure whose motives are constantly debated.

extendedContent.title: Legacy and Interpretations
extendedContent.content: Clytemnestra's legacy is complex and multifaceted. She is often portrayed as a villain, a woman driven by uncontrollable rage and a thirst for revenge. However, modern interpretations also recognize her as a victim of circumstance, a woman whose actions were shaped by the patriarchal society in which she lived and the horrific sacrifice of her daughter. Her story has been retold and reinterpreted in numerous works of literature, theater, and film, each offering a different perspective on her motives and actions. From Aeschylus's 'Oresteia' to contemporary adaptations, Clytemnestra continues to fascinate and provoke debate. She represents the complexities of justice, revenge, and the roles of women in ancient Greek society. Her enduring presence in popular culture serves as a reminder of the enduring power of myth and its ability to reflect and challenge our own values and beliefs. She stands as a symbol of female rage and the consequences of unchecked power.

shortDescription: Clytemnestra, wife of Agamemnon, avenged her daughter's sacrifice by murdering her husband upon his return from the Trojan War. A figure of vengeance and female rage.
